text: This is a total laparoscopic Whipple procedure with an interesting reconstruction.
The patient is positioned in Trendelenburg, and the surgeon initiates the procedure using a medial approach to access the duodenum.
The surgeon fully mobilizes the duodenum, completing the process of Kocherization to expose the duodenum and surrounding structures.
Here's the posterior surface.
Following the cocharization of the duodenum, the surgeon identifies the groove of the superior mesenteric vein, visible as a distinct anatomical landmark.
The surgeon identifies the bowel just distal to the ligament of Treitz and divides it using a surgical stapler, visibly separating the bowel segment.
The surgeon delivers the bowel into the right upper quadrant, positioning it for further surgical manipulation.
With the patient positioned in reverse Trendelenburg, the surgeon completes the mobilization of the hepatic flexure, allowing for better access to the surgical field.
The surgeon opens the lesser sac to expose the anterior surface of the pancreas, then identifies and skeletonizes the superior mesenteric vein, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies and isolates the gastrocolic trunk, visually separating it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon completes the division of the bowel distal to the ligament of Treitz using a surgical stapler.
The surgeon reveals the tunnel posterior to the pancreatic neck, clearly identifying the confluence of veins in the surgical field.
The surgeon inserts a Nathanson liver retractor to elevate the liver, facilitating superior dissection above the pancreas.
The surgeon divides the stomach just proximal to the pylorus, separating it from the duodenum as part of the pancreaticoduodenectomy procedure.
The pancreas is clearly visible in the surgical field, with surrounding structures and tissues exposed for further dissection.
The surgeon skeletonizes and identifies the hepatic artery and gastroduodenal artery, clearly exposing these vessels in the surgical field.
The surgeon ties off and divides the gastroduodenal artery after confirming its anatomical position, with the surrounding structures clearly visible.
The surgeon has developed a tunnel posterior to the pancreatic neck, revealing the anatomical space behind the pancreas.
The surgeon uses a back scratcher instrument to confirm the presence of the tunnel posterior to the pancreatic neck, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses the Thunder Beat device to divide the pancreatic neck, which is clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon estimates the location of the pancreatic duct and cuts it sharply. The remainder of the pancreatic neck transection is completed using the Thunder Beat device.
The superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and portal vein confluence are clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon begins to identify and isolate the bile duct, carefully dissecting around the structure to separate it from surrounding tissues.
The lateral view shows the bile duct with a lymph node being carefully dropped down, revealing the spatial relationship between these structures.
The right hepatic artery is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a bulldog clamp to control the bile duct before proceeding to divide it.
This helps prevent spillage of excessive bile during the operation.
The surgeon divides the bile duct while preserving the right hepatic artery, which remains visible in the surgical field.
The uncinate process remains attached to the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and superior mesenteric vein (SMV), visible as the final point of attachment in the surgical field.
The surgical field shows the lesion positioned at a safe distance from the superior mesenteric artery (SMA), ensuring no immediate risk of vascular involvement.
The surgeon leaves a small cuff of tissue near the uncinate process intact and uses surgical staplers to divide and separate the uncinate process from surrounding structures.
The surgeon places the resected specimen into a specimen retrieval bag, preparing to extract it through a fan and steel incision for frozen section analysis.
The surgeon performs a cholecystectomy, removing the gallbladder and placing it into a separate specimen retrieval bag for extraction.
The surgeon sharply divides the pancreas at the level of the duct, although the duct is not clearly visible in this case.
The surgical field appears clean and dry, indicating the completion of resection steps, as the surgeon prepares to begin the reconstruction phase of the pancreaticoduodenectomy.
The surgeon mobilizes the pancreas, carefully separating it from the branches of the celiac artery and the splenic vein located posteriorly.
The surgeon deploys stay sutures along the marginal vessels located at the inferior and superior edges of the pancreas, securing these vessels in preparation for further surgical steps.
Due to the difficulty in visualizing the pancreatic duct, the surgeon prepares to perform a pancreatic gastrostomy by connecting the pancreas to the posterior wall of the stomach.
The surgeon performs a posterior gastrotomy using the Thunderbeat device, creating an incision in the posterior wall of the stomach.
The surgeon places a purse string suture around the pancreatic tissue, taking full-thickness bites to ensure there are no gaps in the suture line.
The surgeon uses the Thunderbeat device to create a small gastrotomy by making a nick in the staple line, revealing the underlying tissue layers.
A Maryland dissector is introduced through the anterior gastrotomy and then passed through the posterior gastrotomy, navigating the surgical field within the stomach.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to pull the stay sutures, facilitating the invagination of the pancreas remnant into the posterior wall of the stomach for the pancreatic gastrostomy.
The surgeon ties down the purse string suture tightly around the pancreas remnant, which has been pulled through the gastrotomy into the stomach, securing the pancreatic gastrostomy.
The surgeon closes the more anteriorly placed gastrotomy using sutures, ensuring the incision is securely sealed.
The surgeon orients the bowel laterally from the right flank in preparation for the hepatico-jejunostomy, ensuring proper alignment for the anastomosis.
The surgeon performs a single-layer hand-sewn technique using a monofilament 5-0 suture to secure the pancreatic gastrostomy.
The surgeon internalizes the stitch and proceeds with a single layer ductomucosa anastomosis, connecting the pancreatic duct to the mucosa.
The completed anastomosis is visually sound and well-oriented, with the structures aligned appropriately.
The surgeon uses a clean white sponge to check for any biliary leak, confirming that there is no visible leakage in the surgical area.
The surgeon sets up an anti-colic gastrojejunostomy, positioning the bowel appropriately for the anastomosis.
The surgeon creates a common channel by firing a surgical stapler, joining the structures with precise alignment.
The surgeon closes the gastroenterostomy in two layers using running braided 3-0 sutures, ensuring secure approximation of the tissue edges.
The surgeon places the final stitch to complete the closure of the anastomosis.
The anastomosis looks pretty good.
This patient did well.
